Title: Police seek public help to find missing 16-year-old girl in Temerloh
Article:
KUANTAN: Police are seeking public assistance to locate a 16-year-old girl who has been missing since May 14 in Temerloh.
In a statement today, acting Jerantut district police chief Deputy Superintendent Arshad Ayob said a missing person report on Ainul Maisarah Abu Bakar was lodged by her father about 10am yesterday.
"The teenager was last seen at Kampung Asli Pasu, Kuala Krau, Temerloh, wearing her school uniform," he said.
He said the girl has a dark complexion and a slim, tall build.
Members of the public with information are urged to contact the Jerantut district police headquarters' District Control Centre at 09-2662222 or visit the nearest police station.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Identity verification required for sponsored ads on social media
Article:
MELAKA: All advertisers using sponsored advertisements on social media platforms will be required to verify their identities in a bid to curb online fraud, Deputy Communications Minister Teo Nie Ching said.
She said the measure, which also targets digital gambling activities and the misuse of prominent individuals' identities, is among the new responsibilities under the Risk Mitigation Code (RMC).
She said the code will come into force tomorrow under the Online Safety Act 2025 (ONSA) and will apply to social media platform providers classified as licensees, namely those with more than eight million users in Malaysia.
"We have seen many sponsored posts using the images of well-known individuals to promote content such as dubious investments, financial scams and online gambling, and so far it has been difficult to take action because we do not know who actually paid for the advertisements," she said.
She said this to reporters after attending the launch of the Kota Melaka Parliamentary Elected Representative for the People (WRUR) programme for the Banda Hilir state constituency at Taman Kota Laksamana Cheng Ho today.
Teo said the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) will require every advertiser, whether an individual or a registered entity, to undergo identity verification by the platform before being allowed to publish sponsored advertisements.
She urged social media platform providers to fully cooperate in complying with the RMC as well as the Child Protection Code (CPC), which will also take effect tomorrow, to ensure the safety of internet users.
On the age-verification mechanism that will also take effect tomorrow to prevent individuals under the age of 16 from opening social media accounts, Teo said official documents such as identity cards or passports will be required for registration.
She acknowledged that some parents are still looking for the best approach to convince their children about the safety measures, but said the majority fully support the initiative.
"Parents are indeed very supportive because they understand that the internet presents many issues such as cyberbullying, sexual abuse and child grooming, all of which are concerns faced by parents," she said.
She said the government will provide a reasonable period for platform providers to fulfil these new responsibilities, but a high level of compliance is expected.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Odeon Cinema fire sparks wave of nostalgic recollections
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Long before the era of multiplexes, reclining seats and online bookings, the Odeon Cinema was a gathering place where generations of moviegoers came together for entertainment, family outings and cherished memories.
Although the building was gutted by fire yesterday, its heyday continues to live on in the memories of those who once filled its halls. Many recall a cinema experience far removed from today's modern standards, but rich in character and nostalgia.
For social media user Cloud Go, the former cinema brings back memories of a simpler time when affordability mattered more than comfort.
Go recalled purchasing third-class tickets for just 40 sen and sitting so close to the screen that moviegoers had to rest their necks against the backs of their chairs to watch comfortably.
"The conditions were basic. Children would squat on the floor, and rats could sometimes be seen running around. But despite all that, it was a nostalgic experience," the user said.
The Odeon Cinema also holds a special place in another social media user, Zara Zulkifli's, family history.
"It was an old-fashioned cinema with its own distinct smell and worn-out interior. It was nothing like the modern cineplexes we have in shopping malls today, but that was what going to the movies was like back then."
Laura Lau shared similar sentiments, recalling how her aunt would take her to the cinema for inexpensive late-night screenings during her childhood.
She also remembered occasions when renowned Hong Kong actors and actresses appeared on stage for live performances, leaving lasting impressions that remain vivid decades later.
"Those memories are truly precious. I can still remember them very clearly," she said.
For Visnu Varmen, the building's significance extended beyond its Odeon identity.
He remembered it as the home of Vennai Cinema, a popular venue for Tamil and other Indian-language films.
"It was where we watched many of our favourite movies. So many beautiful memories were made there," he said.
Among the films he remembered were those starring Vijay (C. Joseph Vijay), who has since become Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu.
While the building no longer serves its original purpose, recollections of former patrons highlight its enduring role as more than just a cinema.
For many, it was a cultural landmark where families bonded, friendships were formed and unforgettable movie-going experiences unfolded — a reminder of an era when the magic of the silver screen often mattered more than the comfort of the seat.
It was reported that a
fire broke out at the former Odeon Cinema building along Penang Road last night
, sending firefighters rushing to contain the blaze at one of the state's most recognisable heritage landmarks.
The building, which housed the Midnight Black nightclub, was engulfed in flames, with damage estimated at 70 per cent. The premises has since been closed for renovation works.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thousands join Wesak Day rites at temples nationwide
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: In a spirit of peace and gratitude, Wesak Day was celebrated across the country today, reflecting Malaysia's multi-racial and multi-religious society.
In Perak, Ipoh's Wat Sribunya Manggarama chairman Datuk Ching Thoo anticipates that more than 5,000 devotees will visit the temple.
He said the celebrations, which fall on a weekend, offer devotees a timely opportunity to attend religious ceremonies and activities organised at the temple.
"Devotees from Ipoh and Tambun are among the largest groups coming to the temple to perform religious rites and commemorate important events in the life of Buddhism's founder, Gautama Buddha," he told Bernama today.
He said Wesak Day is observed to commemorate three significant events in the life of Gautama Buddha; his birth, enlightenment and passing.
In Sarawak, around 3,000 Buddhists gathered at the Sarawak Buddhist Association temple in Kuching for a prayer ceremony, which was also attended by Deputy Premier and state Public Health, Housing and Local Government Minister Datuk Amar Dr Sim Kui Hian.
In Penang, Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow said Wesak Day is more than a religious celebration, as it also carries a universal message of compassion, wisdom, moderation and harmony in society.
Celebrations in the state were held in a simple yet lively atmosphere, with thousands of Buddhists visiting major temples to take part in various religious ceremonies.
Among the main focal points were Wat Chaiya Mangalaram in Lorong Burma, Wat Buppharam in Jalan Perak and Kek Lok Si in Air Itam, which began receiving devotees as early as 8am.
A festive atmosphere was also evident at the Penang Lee Sih Chong Soo (Lee Kongsi) building on Jalan Burma, where devotees of all ages came with their families to light lotus-shaped candles and lamps, offer flowers and take part in the bathing ceremony of the Buddha statue, one of the main highlights of Wesak Day celebrations.
In Perlis, Wesak Day celebrations in Kangar organised by the Perlis Buddhist Society began as early as 8am, with about 800 devotees attending to take part in religious ceremonies.
Perlis Buddhist Society president Dr Tan Chan Sin said attendance is expected to increase later in the evening, while hoping the celebration will continue to further enhance harmony within the community.
He said the celebrations also included the provision of vegetarian meals for visitors.
In Pahang, Pahang Buddhist Society secretary Wong Sien Biang said about 6,000 Buddhists from within and outside Kuantan are expected to attend the celebrations at Indera Mahkota 8.
"From our observations today, a majority of the visitors were tourists as many were unfamiliar to us. I'd say around 60 per cent were tourists and 40 per cent locals," he said.
Wong said among the key ceremonies held during the Wesak Day celebration were devotional chanting and the Bathing the Little Buddha ceremony.
In Kelantan, Wesak Day at Wat Machimaram, Tumpat, was celebrated in a lively atmosphere, with nearly 1,000 Buddhists and Siamese from within and outside the state in attendance.
Kelantan State Siamese Women's Association chairman KC Ngiam Eh Kuan said the celebration has continued to receive strong support every year.
"Every year, we have a lively celebration, and the Siamese community in Kelantan eagerly looks forward to Wesak Day. Tonight, a religious ceremony will be held at 8.30pm," she said.
In Selangor, thousands of devotees were present to take part in various spiritual and cultural activities at the Fo Guang Shan Dong Zen Temple in Jenjarom, Kuala Langat.
Hiu Li Wei said she was excited to participate in the bathing ceremony of the baby Buddha statue, which symbolises self-purification and commemorates the birth of the Buddha.
"This ceremony brings me peace and reminds me to cleanse my mind of impurities such as hatred and ignorance," she said.
Tam Choi Man, 40, said this year's celebration felt more meaningful, as the peaceful, flower-decorated temple offered a deep spiritual experience.
"Besides taking part in the bathing ceremony of the Buddha statue, I also spent time in meditation and listened to sutra recitations in the main hall," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Over 100,000 expected to throng Brickfields for Malaysia's largest Wesak Day celebration
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: More than 100,000 devotees, tourists and well-wishers are expected to throng the Buddhist Maha Vihara in Brickfields today as Malaysia's largest Wesak Day celebration gets under way with religious observances, charitable initiatives and a grand candlelight procession featuring 35 decorated floats.
The temple's honorary secretary, Tilak Leslie, said preparations for the annual celebration began two months ago to ensure the smooth running of the religious and community activities planned for devotees.
"We expect about 100,000 devotees, tourists and well-wishers to pass through our gates throughout the day, while an estimated 30,000 people will participate in the candlelight procession this evening," he said.
Leslie said the highlight of this year's celebration would be the Wesak candlelight procession, which will feature 35 floats representing the Malaysian, Nepali, Bangladeshi, Thai and Myanmar Buddhist communities.
"We are expecting Transport Minister Anthony Loke and Sri Lankan High Commissioner to Malaysia Darshana Perera to flag off the float procession about 6.30pm," he said at the temple.
Leslie said the temple's programme combines spiritual observances with acts of charity and community service in keeping with the teachings of the Buddha.
"Among the key activities are special prayers, a midnight meditation session, an Eight Precepts programme, blood and organ donation campaigns, a Silent Mentor initiative, sponsorship of 10,000 oil lamps and the construction of a Wesak thorana, a lighted display depicting significant events in the Buddha's life," he said.
He added that the temple is also distributing 10,000 free vegetarian food packs and drinks to visitors throughout the celebration.
The activities lined up, he added, were designed not only to commemorate the birth, enlightenment and passing of the Buddha, but also to encourage devotees to practise compassion, generosity and mindfulness.
He said 60 participants took part in the Eight Precepts programme on both Wesak Eve and Wesak Day, temporarily adopting a more disciplined lifestyle centred on renunciation, mindfulness and harmlessness.
"The precepts include abstaining from killing, stealing, false speech, intoxicants and other worldly indulgences."
Meanwhile, 42 participants joined an overnight meditation programme from midnight until 6am today.
"The meditation session seeks to emulate what the Buddha did more than 2,600 years ago when he attained enlightenment through deep meditation in the stillness of the night," Leslie said.
He said devotees could also receive blessings from monks during special blessing services held throughout the day.
To accommodate the large crowd, Leslie said the temple management had erected canopies in selected areas within its premises and enlisted the assistance of the People's Volunteer Corps (Rela) and St John Ambulance Malaysia to provide crowd management and emergency response services.
"In addition to religious activities, several community outreach programmes are being conducted, including blood donation and organ donation campaigns, the Silent Mentor programme and Green Wesak, an environmental initiative where volunteers collect recyclable waste."
Leslie said the temple hoped this year's Wesak celebration would inspire Malaysians to embrace the values of peace, compassion and goodwill.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Laos cave rescue: Diving is last resort to find remaining miners, says Malaysian diver
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Critical details provided by five rescued survivors are guiding the search for two gold miners that have been trapped inside a flooded cave in central Laos for over a week.
With conditions being hazardous, Malaysian technical cave diver Lee Kian Lie, who is part of the international rescue mission, said that diving is currently the last resort.
"Diving is the last option to search for the remaining victims for now.
"Everyone here is still highly motivated to look for the missing two based on the information given by the surviving five (miners).
"We will try our best," he told the New Straits Times.
The victims were part of a seven-member gold mining expedition in a remote village in Xaysomboun, central Laos, an area known for its limestone caves.
They were trapped in the cave on May 20, after heavy rain flooded the area and choked their exit passage.
Although the rescue team is still in good spirits to continue the mission, pumping out the floodwaters remains one of the mission's biggest challenges, Lee added.
Lee said the five who were rescued are in good health and currently in the hospital receiving treatment.
Lee, who has 13 years of experience in cave exploration was invited to join the rescue team.
Previously, Bernama reported that the search had been hampered by narrow passages, zero visibility, and worsening weather conditions.
Rescuers located five
of the seven miners on Wednesday.
One was brought out safely on May 29, while four others emerged from the cave on May 30.
Two remain missing, trapped about 300m underground.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US chemical spill death toll rises to 11
Article:
LOS ANGELES: The death toll from a chemical spill at a paper plant in Washington state has risen to 11 as rescuers recovered the bodies of all nine missing people, US authorities said on Saturday.
A huge tank holding tens of thousands of gallons of a highly caustic substance imploded on Tuesday at the plant in Longview, Washington state, sparking a major search operation.
"We have recovered the ninth and final missing employee of this incident," Brad Hannig, fire chief of Longview Fire Department, told a news conference.
Rescuers said on Wednesday that two people had died while nine others remained missing.
The accident at the Nippon Dynawave Packaging Company happened during an early morning shift change when a 3.4 million-litre tank containing a large quantity of a substance called white liquor ruptured.
White liquor is a highly alkaline solution containing sodium hydroxide and sodium sulfide that is used to break down wood chips and create the pulp from which paper is made.
Nippon Dynawave Packaging, which is a subsidiary of Japan's Nippon Paper Group, says on its website that it produces eight billion single-serve containers every year, supplying customers in North America, Asia and around the world.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Indonesia faces question of structural integrity versus short-term fixes
Article:
QUESTIONS abound as the Indonesian rupiah reaches new depths. It has descended to levels last seen during the Asian financial crisis of 1997-98 against the United States dollar in recent weeks.
Even against the ringgit, the rupiah fell to a historic low of RM1 to 4,500 rupiah this week.
Ordinary Indonesians have taken to social media and some have been scathing in decrying the economic policies of President Prabowo Subianto whom they blamed for the current state of affairs.
What has brought on all this?
Prabowo has been highly vocal recently, railing against both Indonesian conglomerates as well as foreign investors for profiting unfairly in exploiting the country's natural resources, be they minerals or its vast hectarage of oil palms.
The measures that Prabowo has quite hastily announced in order to address this – principally by ordering that these commodities will henceforth be exported through a single government-linked entity – have caused alarm in the business community.
While the Indonesian leader has been careful to stress that his country welcomes investments, his very actions and public utterances probably have had the opposite effect as investors verged on near-panic and headed for the exits, exacerbating the flight from the rupiah into safe-haven currencies such as the US dollar.
Prabowo is of course right in highlighting the perennial problem of corruption and collusion of businesses in it.
Instead of being seen to be attacking this at source by rounding up corrupt officials, the blame appears to have been shifted to businesses for their collusion in corruption as well as through tax avoidance by channelling export revenues to offshore companies in jurisdictions with less onerous tax regimes.
But these are rather entrenched problems that likely defy easy and short-term drastic official actions to address them.
Good practices such as respecting Indonesian laws and encouraging businesses from skirting or outright disregarding them must necessarily be a long-term objective.
The seeming "shock-and-awe" methods favoured by Prabowo may have generated the opposite of what is intended if investors decide to stay away instead.
Prabowo may also have been motivated to take stern action because of concerns that Indonesia's budget deficit may be widening, again principally through his own action in implementing an election promise of free meals for school children across the vast archipelago.
Critics have pointed out that implementing the free-meals programme will be hugely expensive and unaffordable under Indonesia's current economic circumstances.
The hope of the Indonesian government must be that its revenues can be quickly boosted by a single agency collecting the country's export earnings.
All this perhaps understandably alarms investors and analysts alike that Indonesia is engineering a back-door nationalisation of the key pillars of its economy.
The country and especially its president have their job cut out to reassure both local and foreign players that this is not the case.
Investors and even knowledgeable ordinary Indonesians need calm, honest and transparent telling by their government that it knows exactly what it is doing and that whatever current hiccups faced are only temporary.
Public confidence is most precious given all the headwinds Indonesia is currently facing.
* The writer views developments in the nation, region and the wider world from his vantage point in Kuching
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Wesak Day draws foreign visitors impressed by cultural, spiritual experience
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The sights, sounds and traditions of Wesak Day drew not only Buddhist devotees but also visitors from abroad, many of whom described the celebration as a unique cultural and spiritual experience.
For Australian tourist Lean Mitchell, 32, who was visiting Malaysia for the first time, witnessing the annual celebration at a Buddhist temple left a lasting impression.
"I've attended cultural festivals in different countries, but this feels very peaceful and meaningful.
"The atmosphere is calm despite the large crowd, and everyone seems genuinely focused on compassion and kindness," she said.
Mitchell said she was particularly moved by the candle-lighting ceremony and the sight of devotees offering prayers and flowers.
"It gives you a chance to pause and reflect. Even as a non-Buddhist, I can appreciate the values being shared here," she said when met outside the Brickfields Buddhist Maha Vihara temple here today.
Another visitor, Kenji Tanaka, 45, from Japan, said he was fascinated by the multicultural nature of the celebration.
"In Japan, we also commemorate the Buddha's birth, but seeing people from different ethnic backgrounds gathering together here is very special. It shows how religion can unite communities," he said, sharing his experience.
Tanaka attended the celebration with his wife and daughter. He added that volunteers at the temple were welcoming and eager to explain the significance of various rituals.
Among the devotees was accountant Jackie Cheong, 38, from Setapak, who has been attending Wesak Day celebrations with his family since childhood.
"Wesak is the most important day in the Buddhist calendar. It reminds us of the Buddha's teachings on compassion, wisdom and self-discipline," he said.
Cheong said the annual observance was also an opportunity to strengthen family bonds and engage in charitable activities.
"Beyond prayers, we are encouraged to help those in need and practise kindness in our daily lives."
Retiree Sim Siew Lan, 68, from Petaling Jaya, described the celebration as a time for personal reflection and gratitude.
"Every year, I come to the temple to offer prayers and reflect on the blessings I have received. Last year, I made my offerings at the Buddhist temple in Petaling Jaya, so this year I came here.
"I normally alternate between these two temples for Wesak," she said when met.
Sim said seeing young people and visitors taking an interest in the celebration in this modern day was also encouraging.
"It shows that the message of peace and compassion remains relevant regardless of age, nationality or background."
As devotees filled temple grounds with prayers, offerings and acts of charity, Wesak Day once again highlighted the enduring appeal of the Buddha's teachings.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Foreigner killed in Odeon building fire classified as sudden death case
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Police have classified the death of a 56-year-old foreign worker, whose body was found following a fire at the former Odeon Cinema building along Penang Road here last night, as a sudden death case.
Northeast district deputy police chief Superintendent Lee Swee Sake said police received a report from the public about 8.50pm regarding the fire.
He said firefighters brought the blaze under control before the victim's body was discovered near the front entrance of the premises at 10.23pm.
"Investigations revealed that the premises was undergoing renovation works to be converted into a restaurant.
"The victim is believed to have been employed by the contractor carrying out the renovation works at the premises," he said in a statement today.
Lee said investigations into the incident were ongoing.
"The case has been classified as a sudden death, and a sudden death report has been opened to facilitate investigations.
"Members of the public are advised not to speculate on the incident as it could affect police investigations into the cause of the victim's death," he said, adding that the Fire and Rescue Department is investigating the cause of the blaze.
It was reported that a Myanmar construction worker was killed in a fire at the former
Odeon Cinema
building.
The building, which now houses the Midnight Black nightclub, was closed for renovation works at the time of the incident.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Victim in Penang Odeon building fire identified
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Myanmar construction worker killed in a fire that gutted the two-storey former Odeon Cinema building along Penang Road here last night has been identified as Mohamad Boshi Sabi Ullah.
Boshi, in his 50s, suffered first-degree burns.
Penang Fire and Rescue Department director Mohamad Shoki Hamzah said the victim was found during firefighting operations at the historic building, which now operates as the Midnight Black entertainment centre.
"The victim was confirmed dead and had suffered first-degree burns," he said today.
Shoki said the victim's remains had been handed over to police for further action.
It was reported that one person was killed in a fire that broke out at the former
Odeon Cinema
building.
The building was closed for renovation works at the time of the incident.
Shoki also said the department received a distress call about 8.50pm.
He said the fire involved a two-storey permanent entertainment outlet measuring about 50ft by 300ft, with an estimated 70 per cent of the premises destroyed.
He said the premises was not operating at the time of the incident, while its manager was not inside the building when the fire broke out.
"Firefighters brought the blaze under control at 10.28pm, but overhaul operations are still ongoing as of this morning due to extensive structural collapse.
"The large-scale collapse of the roof structure and concrete walls has restricted access to the central section of the premises for cooling and overhaul works.
"The fibre-based wall materials also contributed to the spread of the fire, and smoke is still present at the location," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Teen among five arrested over viral brawl near Port Klang apartments
Article:
SHAH ALAM: An 18-year-old was among five men arrested for their suspected involvement in a brawl near the Seri Perantau Apartments in Port Klang yesterday.
The incident was captured in a video that later went viral on social media.
South Klang district police chief Assistant Commissioner Lim Jit Huey said police received a report on the incident about 4pm yesterday.
"Following the report, five suspects aged between 18 and 47 were arrested to assist in the investigation.
"All the suspects have been remanded for four days from today, and the cause of the fight is still under investigation.
"The case is being investigated under Section 148 of the Penal Code for rioting while armed with a weapon or an object capable of causing injury," he said in a statement today.
He added that those found guilty could face imprisonment, a fine, or both.
Lim also advised the public against speculating or spreading unverified information on social media, as it could cause public anxiety and affect the ongoing investigation.
Earlier, a video showing a group of individuals involved in a brawl went viral on social media.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] KLIA Aerotrain resumes operations on June 1, says Transport Minister
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Aerotrain at Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA) Terminal 1 will resume full 24-hour operations on June 1, Transport Minister Anthony Loke said.
The resumption follows the completion of a Comprehensive Action Plan implemented in coordination with the Land Public Transport Agency (Apad) and verified by independent railway assessors.
"The Comprehensive Action Plan, undertaken since November 2025, covered three structured stages: inspection and rectification works, system testing and validation, and trial operations," he said in a statement today.
Loke added that endurance testing was conducted over 24-hour and 48-hour periods under both passenger and non-passenger conditions.
"Since January 2026, the Aerotrain has recorded 100 per cent operational service availability," he said.
However, daily engineering hours from midnight to 5am will be maintained, during which a single train will continue serving passengers while routine inspection and maintenance works are carried out.
He said the ministry, through Apad, will continue to exercise regulatory oversight of the Aerotrain's operations.
"Malaysia Airports remains accountable for maintaining the service standards established under this plan," Loke said.
The action plan, which commenced on Nov 15, 2025, was led by
Malaysia Airports Holdings Bhd
and required coordination with contractors Alstom and the IJMC-Pestech joint venture, with support from project consultants and independent technical assessors.
It was previously reported that the Dewan Rakyat was told last year that the
Aerotrain at KLIA Terminal 1
recorded 19 incidents between July 2 and Sept 30.
The incidents were attributed to various issues, including signalling problems and human-related incidents, such as passengers forcing open train doors.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Khaled tempers expectations of compensation from Norway over missile deal
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The compensation linked to the cancellation of Malaysia's Naval Strike Missile (NSM) procurement will be brought by the Norwegian Defence Minister Tore O. Sandvik to his government for consideration.
Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin said the matter was raised during bilateral discussions with his Norwegian counterpart, who indicated that it would be referred back to Oslo.
"The matter will be brought by the Norwegian Defence Minister to his government. However, whether we will get a favourable outcome or not, I am doubtful.
"We will see what the Norwegian government does with regard to what I have conveyed. If it truly wants to maintain good relations and preserve the friendship, it may take action to prioritise (the compensation) for what we have paid, and it is for them and their company to settle between themselves in recovering what they had advanced to us," he said.
The revocation of the export licence for the NSM had previously drawn strong objections from Malaysia, with the missile system earmarked for the Royal Malaysian Navy's Littoral Combat Ship (LCS) and the rearming of the Lekiu-class frigate programmes.
Khaled also said that Malaysia has sent a letter of demand to the company over the issue.
Norway was reported to have withdrawn several export licences related to certain technologies as part of efforts to tighten the country's export controls, which affected the NSM procurement agreement between the RMN and the manufacturer of the NSM system.
That deal was signed in April 2018 with a contract value of €124 million (RM571.9 million) to equip then six new LCS.
It was reported on May 19 that Malaysia had issued a notice of demand to a Norwegian defence firm over the cancellation of an export licence for a missile system intended for the navy's LCS, claiming losses exceeding
RM1 billion
.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man loses over RM500,000 in online investment scam
Article:
MIRI: A man's dream of earning lucrative returns through an online investment turned into a nightmare after he lost RM511,406 to a non-existent scheme.
The ordeal started when the victim, in his 50s, received a WhatsApp message from an unknown number on March 18, before being introduced to an investment scheme that promised high returns in a short period.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Farhan Lee Abdullah said the victim followed a link provided by the suspect and was instructed to make several payments into designated bank accounts for the supposed investment.
He said 25 transactions were made to various accounts between April 1 and May 24.
"The victim made 25 transactions amounting to RM511,406. However, after the money was transferred, the suspect continued to demand additional payments, claiming they were needed for the victim to receive the promised investment profits," he said.
Farhan said the victim never received any profits or returns as promised and eventually realised he had been cheated. He lodged a police report yesterday.
The case is being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code for cheating.
The public is advised to remain vigilant and not be easily deceived by investment advertisements promising high returns in a short period.
They are also encouraged to verify such schemes with the relevant authorities and contact the National Scam Response Centre (NSRC) at 997 if they fall victim to cyber fraud.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Seremban street racing: Cops seize 19 motorcycles, arrest four in Senawang operation
Article:
SEREMBAN: Police issued 209 summonses for various offences after checking 322 vehicles and individuals during a six-hour integrated operation targeting motorcyclists along Jalan Persiaran Senawang 1 here last night.
Seremban district police chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Yatim Osman said the operation, which began at 10pm, was carried out with several enforcement agencies and targeted hotspots for illegal street racing.
He said offences included modified exhaust systems, registration numbers that did not meet specifications, no registration plates and side mirrors, as well as riding without a valid driving licence or insurance coverage.
"We also seized 19 motorcycles and arrested four men aged between 16 and 37 under Section 42(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 and Section 45A(1) of the same Act for riding under the influence of alcohol," he told reporters after the operation early this morning.
He said one motorcyclist attempted to escape but was apprehended.
Yatim said checks also found some people had come to watch and support groups suspected of being involved in illegal racing.
"We are not only checking the racers but also those who come to support and encourage them, as they indirectly provide a platform for the organisation of activities that violate the law," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sri Lanka raise fuel prices after IMF loan instalment
Article:
COLOMBO: Sri Lanka raised fuel prices by up to six percent on Sunday, in line with IMF plans to recover energy costs and phase out subsidies to stabilise the economy.
Petrol was raised to 434 rupees (US$1.33), up from 410, while diesel increased to 407 rupees a litre from 392, the state-run Ceylon Petroleum Corp said.
The price hike came days after the International Monetary Fund released a US$695 million instalment of a US$2.9 billion bailout loan, agreed in early 2023 to stabilise the cash-strapped South Asian nation.
The IMF wants Sri Lanka to ensure cost recovery for both fuel and electricity tariffs, which have been subsidised by the government since the start of the conflict in the Middle East in February.
President Anura Kumara Dissanayake, in a letter to the IMF made public by the Washington-based international lender, said fuel subsidies will be phased out by September.
Since the United States and Israel began attacking Iran on February 28, triggering a global energy crisis, Sri Lanka has raised petrol and diesel prices by about 48 percent. Electricity has increased by a third.
The Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway through which about 20 per cent of global oil exports pass in peacetime, has been effectively closed by Iran.
Sri Lanka imports all its oil and also buys coal for electricity generation.
Colombo has warned that the fighting in the Middle East, and any prolonged conflict, could seriously undermine its efforts to emerge from the economic meltdown of 2022.
Sri Lanka defaulted on its US$46 billion foreign debt in 2022 after running out of foreign exchange. Since then, Colombo has been drawing down the IMF bailout loan to stabilise the country.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Energy crunch fuels car pool growth
Article:
NEW DELHI: Rising fuel prices triggered by the Middle East war are driving a sharp increase in carpooling, with a ride-sharing platform reporting a surge in new users seeking cheaper ways to travel.
The world's largest carpooling platform BlaBlaCar said soaring energy costs have pushed 600,000 additional drivers onto the app this year – 20 per cent more than initially projected – as commuters look to offset the rising cost of fuel.
In India, its single biggest market with more than 20 million users in 2025, the number of passengers has increased by 40 per cent since the start of the US-Israeli airstrikes against Iran on Feb 28.
Last year, the global carpooling leader posted record-breaking figures in the world's most populous country India – outpacing Brazil with 19 million users and France with seven million, according to Benjamin Retourne, the platform's product director.
This trend has been more pronounced in countries where fuel price increases driven by the war have been sudden and significant, combined with limited government support, such as in France.
The platform works by connecting drivers and passengers willing to travel together between cities to share costs, with the app in most of its 21 operating nations taking a 20 per cent commission.
Saves fuel
Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi earlier in May urged the country's 1.4 billion citizens to save fuel by making greater use of carpooling and public transport.
India imports approximately half of its crude oil via the Strait of Hormuz, which Teheran effectively closed in retaliation to US-Israeli strikes launched in February.
Retourne said a decade ago, when BlaBlaCar first launched in India, even "after two or three years, it just wasn't catching on."
The company, founded in France in 2006, therefore stopped investing in the world's fastest growing economy but kept its application running from its Paris headquarters, unlike many large foreign groups that outsourced their services to India.
Growth finally began to pick up after the Covid-19 pandemic, driven by the country's economic and digital acceleration, as well as word of mouth.
Retourne pointed to a growth in private car ownership as well as rapid urbanisation, with 200 million additional city dwellers over the past decade.
"People are very connected," he said, adding that "today, wherever you go, there is 5G."
That proved to be the "recipe for carpooling to take off."
But he was surprised that a driving factor was not the cost – but rather to avoid often crowded buses or trains.
"The number one reason people choose carpooling is not price, but comfort", he said.
The market potential remains vast, with "a new segment of the population" eager to travel for leisure and visit friends and family.
'Saves time'
Assistant bank manager Pratyush Anuraj, 24, from India's financial capital Mumbai, said he used the carpooling platform to travel to his family's home in Pune every weekend – a 150km journey of around 2.5 hours.
"It's cheaper than the train, the bus, or a private taxi," he said. "It also saves time, as there are few stops and the vehicle doesn't wait beyond the scheduled time."
He does, however, point out some drawbacks: either the trip is often cancelled at the last minute, or the drivers don't answer calls.
So far, the French platform has not monetised carpooling in India, where people pay each other directly, often using the popular digital UPI mobile telephones payment systems.
It now wants to evolve its model.
The next objective is to "build a platform that aggregates multiple modes of transport" – connecting cars to buses and trains.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar: Reject racial, religious hatred through compassion
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has called on Malaysians to embrace the universal values of love and compassion highlighted during Wesak Day and reject attempts by certain quarters to incite racial and religious animosity.
Anwar said the spread of love and compassion would drown out hatred, dispel prejudice, and strengthen mutual respect and understanding, which form the foundation of national harmony and unity.
"In a nation of diverse cultures, religions and backgrounds, the values of love, compassion and benevolence highlighted during Wesak Day celebrations hold profound significance, particularly at a time when certain quarters are attempting to fan the flames of racial and religious animosity.
"I believe that the spreading of love and compassion will drown out the incitement of hatred, clear prejudices, and strengthen mutual respect and understanding, which serve as the foundation of national harmony and unity," he said in a statement today.
He said these values were not only celebrated within religious traditions but also remained deeply relevant in building a society rooted in mutual understanding and respect.
"May this year's Wesak Day celebration bring peace, prosperity and happiness to all Buddhists, and continue to foster the human values that unite us as a civilised and dignified nation," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Increase in special grant for Sabah shows responsible federal government, says Hajiji
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The increase in Sabah's interim special grant to RM1.5 billion is proof that the federal government under Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim's leadership is a responsible administration that delivers on its promises.
Chief Minister Datuk Seri Hajiji Noor said the announcement by Anwar yesterday was greatly appreciated, particularly the prime minister's commitment to continue respecting and implementing the Malaysia Agreement 1963 (MA63).
In welcoming the announcement, Hajiji said the state government hopes the allocation can be disbursed immediately for the benefit of the people.
"This demonstrates that the federal government under the leadership of Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim is a responsible administration that fulfils its promises," he said in a statement today.
Hajiji said the assurance given by the prime minister during the opening of the 2026 state-level Kaamatan Festival reflected the federal government's continued commitment to safeguarding Sabah's constitutional rights.
He stressed that the state government remains committed to working closely with the federal government to ensure that all allocations and entitlements under MA63, particularly the 40 per cent special grant, are fully realised.
Yesterday, Anwar announced that Sabah's interim special grant would be increased to RM1.5 billion this year, up from RM600 million previously.
The prime minister said the increase is an interim measure while the mechanism to implement Sabah's 40 per cent net revenue entitlement is being finalised through formal negotiations between the federal and state governments.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man arrested over attempted house break-in caught on viral video
Article:
KLUANG: Police arrested a 43-year-old man in connection with an attempted house break-in that was caught in a video clip that went viral on social media.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner Bahrin Mohd Noh said the suspect was arrested at Jalan Kampung SC here about 12.45pm yesterday.
The case was first detected after a video circulated online showed a man attempting to enter a house at Lorong Arnab, Kampung Padang Tembak, about 4pm on May 27.
A report was lodged later by a woman claiming the suspect attempted to force open her house door.
Bahrin said the suspect had a prior criminal record, while a urine screening returned negative for drugs.
"A remand order was obtained today to keep the suspect in police custody to facilitate investigations under Section 457 of the Penal Code for house-breaking and Section 511 for attempted break-in, both of which carry jail terms and fines upon conviction," Bahrin said in a statement today.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: B15 biodiesel rollout starts Monday
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is set to enter a new phase in its energy transition amid a global supply crisis, with the biodiesel blend rate in Peninsular Malaysia to be gradually raised from B10 to B15 starting Monday, June 1.
The move by the Madani Government in the face of global energy market volatility is aimed not only at reducing reliance on imported fossil diesel, but is also seen as a two-pronged strategy to enhance energy security and increase demand for locally produced palm oil.
The implementation will see B15 biodiesel comprising 15 per cent palm oil-based biodiesel, known as Palm Methyl Ester (PME), and 85 per cent petroleum diesel, compared with the existing B10 blend of 10 per cent PME, without requiring engine modifications for most diesel vehicles.
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi previously said the government was targeting biodiesel production in stages as an initial step before the blend rate is increased according to market suitability and industry capabilities.
He said that by-products from the palm oil industry, including sludge from crude palm oil production, have the potential to be used in biodiesel and aviation fuel production, creating a new value chain in the country's commodity sector.
Ahmad Zahid also views biodiesel as a new catalyst for the rural economy, through the involvement of agencies such as the Federal Land Consolidation and Rehabilitation Authority (Felcra), the Central Terengganu Development Authority (Ketengah) and the South Kelantan Development Authority (Kesedar).
The government has assured that the implementation will be carried out in a planned manner, taking into account the country's palm oil supply capacity, industrial preparedness and existing infrastructure.
Plantation and Commodities Minister Datuk Seri Dr Noraini Ahmad palm oil usage for B15 implementation is estimated at around 0.8 million tonnes annually, significantly lower than the domestic supply surplus of about four million tonnes per year.
She therefore emphasised that the implementation will not affect the country's palm oil exports, which currently stand at around 16 million tonnes per year.
Meanwhile, University Putra Malaysia Biorefinery Complex head Prof Dr Hidayah Ariffin said higher biodiesel usage could extend the country's diesel stockpiling period and help ensure supply stability if the global energy crisis linked to the West Asia conflict involving the United States, Israel and Iran persists.
Concerns over the suitability of biodiesel for vehicles are also being addressed by the government, with Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir assuring that there are no issues with engine specifications, as Malaysia's vehicle standards already provide for biodiesel blends of up to B20 since 2020.
Akmal Nasrullah said the use of B20 has already been introduced in several areas, including Langkawi, Labuan and Sarawak, without any major issues that could be cited in relation to alleged engine damage.
The National Biodiesel Programme began with B5 in 2011 before being expanded to B7 in 2014, followed by B10 and B20 in stages between 2019 and 2020.
Malaysia currently has 19 licensed biodiesel plants capable of supporting the implementation of B15 and the government's long-term plans to raise the blend rate to B20, B30 and potentially B50, in line with the national green agenda.
In addition, the use of biodiesel produced from renewable sources is expected to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and support Malaysia's commitment to low-carbon development and a more sustainable energy transition.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: King grants hour-long audience to PM after breakfast meeting
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, granted an audience to Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim this morning.
The ruler and Anwar first met over breakfast at a cafe along Jalan Raja Chulan.
"Sultan Ibrahim then held a private audience with Datuk Seri Anwar at Istana Bukit Tunku, which lasted more than an hour," read a post on the King's Facebook page.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Khaled: Norway apologised for cancelling missile deal but stands by decision
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Norway has apologised over the cancellation of Malaysia's naval strike missile (NSM) export licence but maintained its decision to uphold the move.
Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin said the matter was informed during a bilateral meeting with Norwegian Defence Minister Tore O. Sandvik on the sidelines of the International Institute for Strategic Studies (IISS) Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore.
"I met my Norwegian counterpart and he conveyed his apology, but also explained the basis for the cancellation.
"I conveyed frankly and clearly our disappointment and regret over the long, close and good relationship that has existed between Norway and Malaysia.
"I also conveyed that as a country that views Malaysia as a friend and wishes to maintain that friendship, the manner and action taken is not in line with a country that has good relations," he said after the meeting.
The revocation of the export licence had previously drawn strong objections from Malaysia, with the missile system earmarked for the Royal Malaysian Navy's Littoral Combat Ship (LCS) and the rearming of the Lekiu-class frigate programmes.
Khaled also said the issue has marred the long-standing close and good relations between Malaysia and Norway.
He said this as Norway's manner and handling of the matter was not in line with the spirit of a strong friendship.
"The manner and action taken is not in line with a country that has good relations, given that it took a long time before we were informed. We signed the contract in 2018 and were supposed to receive the delivery this year.
"However we were only informed in April, whereas in March, when the missile was supposed to be delivered we were informed that there was a problem.
"At that time (in March), there was no official notification of the cancellation of the export licence," he said.
Khaled added that while Malaysia did not deny that the situation stemmed from Norway's decision to revoke the export licence, the outcome would have been viewed differently had there been efforts to address financial implications of the cancellation.
"We might be able to accept Norway as a friendly country if there had been efforts by the Norwegian government to perhaps repay all that we have paid, to return all the money we have paid, and for action to recover the money from the company to be undertaken by the Norwegian government itself.
"At the very least, whether full payment or partial," he said.
Khaled, however, said he remained doubtful of a favourable resolution, despite the issue of repayment will be brought by his Norwegian counterpart to his government.
"The matter will be brought by the Norwegian defence minister to his government. However, whether we will get a favourable outcome or not, I am doubtful," he said.
Khaled said the episode would leave a lasting impact on Malaysia–Norway relations and could not be easily reversed.
"The absence of all this shows that there is no meaning to the statement that Malaysia–Norway relations are good and long-standing and must be further strengthened, because what we have gone through will become a permanent blemish in
Malaysia–Norway
relations and it can never return to how it was before," he said.
He added that the development would serve as a lesson for Malaysia in assessing future defence partnerships, particularly in determining the sincerity and reliability of partner nations.
"Norway's action will serve as a lesson for us to ensure that in conducting relations, we must properly determine which countries are sincere and genuine, and which are not," he said.
Norway was reported to have withdrawn several export licences related to certain technologies as part of efforts to tighten the country's export controls, which affected the NSM procurement agreement between the RMN and the manufacturer of the NSM system.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Asean must not be drawn into superpower rivalries, says Khaled
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Southeast Asia must not be drawn into great-power rivalry or pressured into choosing sides as geopolitical competition intensifies, says Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin.
"External powers engaging with this region must respect Asean's norms, strategic sensitivities and regional mechanisms, not bypass them," he said, adding that Asean remained anchored in its principle of centrality.
Speaking at the International Institute for Strategic Studies Shangri-La Dialogue plenary session in Singapore, Khaled said Southeast Asia remained a peaceful region largely due to Asean's indispensable role.
"But today, Southeast Asia faces mounting pressure from growing geopolitical rivalries, geoeconomic fragmentation, technological competition and strategic coercion," he said.
Khaled said regional countries were increasingly being pushed to recalibrate their long-standing strategic positions, adding that Asean was built on cooperation and consensus.
"Asean was never built upon coercion. Asean was never built upon dominance and it was never built upon transactional politics.
"It was built upon dialogue, consensus, mutual respect and collective responsibility for regional stability," he said.
Khaled warned that any erosion of Asean centrality would have serious consequences for the region and beyond.
"The moment Asean centrality is compromised, regionalism in Southeast Asia will cease to be meaningful and purposeful.
"It would be extremely damaging not only for Southeast Asia, but also the wider Asia Pacific region and the rest of the world," he said.
Previously, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said Asean must maintain its strong relationships with both the United States and China amid intensifying competition between the two superpowers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pas info chief suggests party contest future polls without Bersatu
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Pas should consider contesting future elections independently of Bersatu, the Islamist party's information chief said amid increasingly strained ties between the two Perikatan Nasional parties.
Ahmad Fadhli Shaari said the two parties could still maintain friendly relations even if they no longer cooperated in elections.
"(This way) you (Bersatu) can find political allies with a better sense of hearing and sight. All the best," Fadhli said in a social media post.
He said harmony between the two parties was no longer possible after a senior Bersatu leader reportedly remarked that anyone claiming the party lacked machinery was "deaf".
It is understood he was referring to a video showing Bersatu secretary-general Datuk Seri Azmin Ali making the remarks during a ceramah in Melaka.
"It is not wrong for a party to lack machinery, especially for a newer party. The issue arises when there is a failure to acknowledge this weakness with humility," he said.
Fadhli said such "denial" could lead to decisions being made based on an inflated sense of confidence.
He said Bersatu's lack of machinery would not be an issue if it were accompanied by efforts to strengthen the party.
"However, if they continue to bicker years after the elections, it does not strengthen the party," he said.
He added that this had contributed to Bersatu's inability to win more seats despite contesting a larger number of constituencies.
"This stems from inaccurate assessments," he said.
Fadhli also pointed to recent developments involving Bersatu in Perlis and Negri Sembilan, which he said had raised concerns over whether the party might seek to form the federal government without Pas after the 16th General Election.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: KL police chief makes surprise visits to two stations
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: City police chief Datuk Fadil Marsus yesterday conducted surprise visits to two police stations, namely the Chow Kit and Tun HS Lee stations.
The visits to the two stations, both of which fall under the Dang Wangi police headquarters, aimed at personally inspecting infrastructure conditions and facilities available to frontline personnel.
Kuala Lumpur police said the surprise working visits were carried out to monitor compliance with standard operating procedures and assess the readiness of officers and rank-and-file personnel.
"The visit was to also assess the condition of infrastructure and station facilities, while looking into the welfare of our personnel," it said in a Facebook post.
It added that the visits provided a platform for leadership to obtain direct feedback from personnel and ensure that policing services are delivered more effectively, professionally, and with integrity.
City police said the visits also enabled senior leadership to better understand the challenges faced by lower-ranking personnel.
Fadil was accompanied during the visits by Tun HS Lee deputy station chief Sub-Inspector Syed Sulaiman and Chow Kit deputy station chief Sub-Inspector Rosman.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hire-purchase revamp starts tomorrow, ensures fairer loan settlements
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sweeping changes to how interest is calculated on hire-purchase loans will come into force tomorrow following the enforcement of the Hire Purchase (Amendment) Act 2026, said Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Minister Datuk Armizan Mohd Ali.
He said the landmark amendments will abolish the flat interest rate and the "Rule of 78" method, replacing them with the reducing balance method and the effective interest rate for fixed-rate hire-purchase loans.
"Under the previous Rule of 78 method, a significant portion of early instalment payments was used to service interest, with only a small fraction allocated toward the principal.
"Under this new method, interest is calculated based on the outstanding loan balance, making early loan settlements more transparent and fair for consumers," Armizan said in a statement today.
He said the amendments also allow the use of electronic signatures and digital document submissions to enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of the agreement process.
Additionally, a requirement for two due diligence checks has been introduced to verify the borrower's identity and mitigate fraud risks.
For the first phase of enforcement, 11 financial institutions and hire-purchase companies will offer the new hire-purchase loans under the amended provisions, with the second phase slated for Sept.
"Consumers wishing to benefit under these new legal provisions are advised to conduct relevant checks before finalising any hire-purchase agreements," Armizan said.
"Financial institutions and hire-purchase companies still in the preparation phase — including system integration and modification — will be granted a grace period until March 31, 2027.
"Financial institutions and hire-purchase companies are encouraged to offer services under this new legal framework as soon as possible and to notify the ministry once their transition process is completed," he said.
Armizan said prior to these amendments, common complaints from the public regarding hire-purchase transactions for vehicle purchases often involved hidden interest charges and high outstanding loan balances, even when early settlements were made.
This situation forced borrowers seeking early settlements to bear high outstanding balances that did not reflect the actual cost of the loan.
He said this legal reform establishes a more transparent and credible credit ecosystem, enhancing industry governance and protecting the rights and interests of consumers nationwide.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Prioritise the maintenance and modernisation of urban rail
Article:
LETTERS:
Malaysia's first-generation Light Rail Transit (LRT) systems, including the Kelana Jaya Line and the Ampang/Sri Petaling Line, were commissioned over two decades ago.
Over the past few years, commuters have experienced repeated service disruptions, signalling failures, train delays, technical breakdowns, and now derailments.
Such incidents not only inconvenience the public but also gradually erode confidence in public transportation.
The latest derailment near Chan Sow Lin LRT Station is another warning sign that our ageing urban rail system is facing reliability and maintenance challenges.
Malaysia has invested heavily in expanding public transport infrastructure through projects such as the MRT and LRT extensions.
However, maintaining and modernising existing systems is equally important.
The government, transport operators, and regulators may therefore need to consider a comprehensive long-term rail asset renewal strategy.
They should include phased replacement of ageing systems, increased maintenance, stronger independent safety oversight and sufficient budgetary allocation for infrastructure renewal.
Commuters understand that technical problems can occasionally occur in any rail system.
What the public expects, however, is assurance that the infrastructure is being responsibly managed for the long term.
Therefore, we should treat the latest derailment to seriously address the sustainability and reliability of the ageing LRT network.
WONG SOO KAN
Petaling Jaya, Selangor
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Don't panic over cannon fire, Putrajaya residents told
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The public, particularly those living near Putrajaya, has been advised not to be alarmed by the sound of cannon fire scheduled for June 3 and 5.
Putrajaya police chief Assistant Commissioner Aidi Sham Mohamed said the sounds would be from ceremonial cannon salutes by the armed forces during the Trooping of the Colours parade for His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, at Dataran Pahlawan Negara in Presint 1, Putrajaya.
Aidi Sham said two ceremonial cannon shots would be fired during the full rehearsal starting about 8.30am on Wednesday (June 3).
"The actual ceremony on Friday (June 5) will feature 21 cannon shots, to be fired at the same time," he said in a statement today.
Aidi Sham said three main roads would be temporarily closed from 7am, namely Persiaran Sultan Salahuddin Abdul Aziz Shah, Lebuh Perdana Timur, and surrounding routes near Complex E.
He said traffic is expected to be heavy in the affected areas between 7am and 10am, and road users are advised to follow police instructions, plan their journeys, and use alternative routes to avoid congestion.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Heavy rain and strong winds forecast for parts of Selangor, Perak, Pahang, and Johor till evening
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 31 — MetMalaysia has issued an urgent thunderstorm warning for parts of four states across peninsular Malaysia until 5pm today.
Affected districts are Kinta, Kampar, Hilir Perak, Batang Padang and Muallim in Perak, Raub and Bentong in Pahang, Hulu Selangor in Selangor, and Mersing and Kota Tinggi in Johor.
All areas are expected to experience thunderstorms, heavy sustained rain and strong winds.
The warning is activated when MetMalaysia detects thunderstorms with rainfall intensity exceeding 20mm per hour that are expected to arrive within one hour.
This is classified as a short term advisory, and is only valid for a maximum of six hours from the time it is issued.
No warnings have been issued for any other state at the time of writing.

Title: Traffic slows on highways in peninsula as holiday volume exceeds two million vehicles
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 31 — Congestion is building on several highways connecting the north, south and east coast regions of peninsular Malaysia today as vehicle numbers increase due to the long weekend as Wesak, the King’s birthday and Aidiladha overlap.
Senior General Manager of Operations at PLUS Malaysia Berhad, Mohd Yusuf Abdul Aziz, said traffic on the North-South Expressway exceeded two million vehicles over the past two days, Berita Harian reported this afternoon.
“For the Northern Region, traffic is slow from Permatang Pauh to Prai, Penang before the Juru Toll Plaza, as well as at Hentian Sungai Bakap heading north, Jawi to Bandar Baharu, Taiping to Changkat Jering, Perak and before the Menora Tunnel towards Ipoh,” he was quoted as saying.
“For the Central Region, traffic is slow after exiting the Sungai Besi Toll Plaza towards the city centre, Gua Tempurung to Gopeng and Nilai to the Seremban Rest Area heading south. On the North–South Expressway Central Link (Elite), traffic is also slow from Putrajaya to Bandar Gamuda Cove and from Bandar Serenia to Nilai, while the Seremban–Port Dickson Highway (SPDH) is slow from Lukut to Port Dickson.”
For the Southern Region, traffic is slow at Hentian Sebelah Senawang to Pedas/Linggi and Yong Peng to Pagoh due to an accident involving two cars at kilometre 141.2 heading north, which has blocked the emergency lane. Traffic is also slow from Sedenak to Kulai and Senai to Kulai.
However, traffic on the Butterworth–Kulim Expressway (BKE), the Penang Bridge and the New Klang Valley Expressway (NKVE) remains smooth.
Mohd Yusuf advised road users to plan their journeys and refer to the MyPlus-TTA app for the latest traffic updates.

Title: Khaled questions Oslo’s commitment to ties with Malaysia as missile refund hangs in balance
Article:
SINGAPORE, May 31 — Malaysia is doubtful it will recover nearly RM543 million already paid to Norway for anti-ship missiles and has delivered a veiled warning that the cancellation could harm their long-standing bilateral relationship.
Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin raised the issue during a bilateral meeting with his Norwegian counterpart on the sidelines of the Shangri-La Dialogue here today,
Bernama
reported.
“We have honestly and clearly expressed our disappointment because a long, close and good relationship has existed between Norway and Malaysia (yet) through this NSM cancellation episode,” he was quoted as saying.
The Naval Strike Missile (NSM) system was procured from Kongsberg Defence and Aerospace in 2018.
Malaysia has sent a formal letter of demand to Norway to refund about 95 per cent of the RM571.9 million contract signed in 2018 to equip six Littoral Combat Ships, and is now waiting for an official reply.
“This matter will be brought by the Norwegian Defence Minister to his government, but whether we will get a favourable decision or not, I am doubtful,” Khaled  was quoted as saying.
“We will see if the Norwegian government really wants to protect this good relationship and maintain the friendship. They might take action to prioritise what we have already paid,” he added.
Norway’s Defence Minister Tore O. Sandvik had earlier conveyed an apology to Malaysia over the export licence cancellation.

Title: Malaysia now requires identity checks for sponsored social media ads, says deputy comms minister
Article:
MELAKA, May 31 — All advertisers using sponsored advertisements on social media platforms will be required to verify identities in a bid to curb online fraud, Deputy Communications Minister Teo Nie Ching said.
She said the measure, which also targets digital gambling activities and the misuse of prominent individuals’ identities, is among the new responsibilities under the Risk Mitigation Code (RMC).
She said the code will come into force today (June 1) under the Online Safety Act 2025 (ONSA) and will apply to social media platform providers classified as licensees, namely those with more than eight million users in Malaysia.
“We have seen many sponsored posts using the images of well-known individuals to promote content such as dubious investments, financial scams and online gambling, and so far it has been difficult to take action because we do not know who actually paid for the advertisements,” she said.
She told reporters this after attending the launch of the Kota Melaka Parliamentary Elected Representative for the People (WRUR) Programme for the Banda Hilir state constituency at Taman Kota Laksamana Cheng Ho here today.
Teo said the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) will require every advertiser, whether an individual or a registered entity, to undergo identity verification by the platform before being allowed to publish sponsored advertisements.
She urged social media platform providers to fully cooperate in complying with the RMC as well as the Child Protection Code (CPC), which will also take effect today, to ensure the safety of internet users.
On the age-verification mechanism that will also take effect today to prevent individuals under the age of 16 from opening social media accounts, Teo explained that official documents such as identity cards or passports will be required for registration purposes.
She acknowledged that some parents are still looking for the best approach to convince their children about the safety measures, but said the majority fully support the initiative.
“Parents are indeed very supportive because they understand that the internet presents many issues such as cyberbullying, sexual abuse and child grooming, all of which are concerns faced by parents,” she said.
She said the government will provide a reasonable period for platform providers to fulfil these new responsibilities, but a high level of compliance is expected. — Bernama

Title: Five men arrested over armed brawl at flat in Port Klang
Article:
SHAH ALAM, May 31 — Police have arrested five local men suspected of being involved in a brawl at Flat Seri Perantau in Port Klang Friday.
Klang Selatan district police chief ACP Lim Jit Huey said the suspects, aged between 18 and 47, were detained after police received a report on the incident at 4pm Friday.
“Acting on information, the Klang Selatan district police headquarters (IPD) arrested five local men. The suspects have been remanded for four days starting yesterday to assist investigations.
“The case is being investigated under Section 148 of the Penal Code, which carries a maximum penalty of five years’ imprisonment or a fine, or both, upon conviction,” he said in a statement, adding that the cause of the fight is still being investigated.
Lim urged members of the public with information to come forward and relay the information to Klang Selatan IPD or any nearby police station to assist investigations.
Earlier, a 2-minute and 41-second video went viral on social media showing a fight involving several individuals, some of whom were seen holding weapons, believed to have taken place at Flat Seri Perantau. — Bernama

Title: MIC respects Perikatan’s revocation of entry approval, says Vigneswaran
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 31 — MIC said it respects Perikatan Nasional’s decision to revoke its earlier approval for the party to join the opposition coalition.
According to
Sinar Harian
, MIC president Tan Sri SA Vigneswaran said the party had no issue with any PN component, including PAS.
“When the offer was made previously, PN’s leadership was led by Bersatu and at that time MIC was indeed prepared to join it.
“However, no final decision was made. When there was a change in leadership, the mandate given by MIC’s grassroots was based on the Bersatu-led leadership at the time,” he was quoted as saying.
Vigneswaran said this after launching MIC’s Family Day celebration in conjunction with the party’s 80th anniversary in Ipoh today.
He also said MIC remains with Barisan Nasional for now despite not being given any role in the unity government, adding that government posts were not the party’s main priority.
On May 17, Sinar Harian reported PN secretary-general Datuk Seri Takiyuddin Hassan as saying the earlier decision to accept MIC into PN was no longer relevant and had been revoked because MIC had not confirmed its entry into the coalition.

Title: B15 biodiesel rollout starts June 1: Why it matters for Malaysia’s energy security
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 31 — Malaysia is set to enter a new phase in its energy transition amid a global supply crisis, with the biodiesel blend rate in Peninsular Malaysia to be gradually raised from B10 to B15 starting tomorrow, June 1.
The move by the Madani Government in the face of global energy market volatility is aimed not only at reducing reliance on imported fossil diesel, but is also seen as a two-pronged strategy to enhance energy security and increase demand for locally produced palm oil.
The implementation will see B15 biodiesel comprising 15 per cent palm oil-based biodiesel, known as Palm Methyl Ester (PME), and 85 per cent petroleum diesel, compared with the existing B10 blend of 10 per cent PME, without requiring engine modifications for most diesel vehicles.
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Ahmad Zahid Hamidi previously said the government was targeting biodiesel production in stages as an initial step before the blend rate is increased according to market suitability and industry capabilities.
He said that by-products from the palm oil industry, including sludge from crude palm oil production, have the potential to be used in biodiesel and aviation fuel production, creating a new value chain in the country’s commodity sector.
Ahmad Zahid also views biodiesel as a new catalyst for the rural economy, through the involvement of agencies such as the Federal Land Consolidation and Rehabilitation Authority (Felcra), the Central Terengganu Development Authority (Ketengah) and the South Kelantan Development Authority (Kesedar).
The government has assured that the implementation will be carried out in a planned manner, taking into account the country’s palm oil supply capacity, industrial preparedness and existing infrastructure.
Plantation and Commodities Minister Datuk Seri Dr Noraini Ahmad palm oil usage for B15 implementation is estimated at around 0.8 million tonnes annually, significantly lower than the domestic supply surplus of about four million tonnes per year.
She therefore emphasised that the implementation will not affect the country’s palm oil exports, which currently stand at around 16 million tonnes per year.
Meanwhile, University Putra Malaysia Biorefinery Complex head Prof Dr Hidayah Ariffin said higher biodiesel usage could extend the country’s diesel stockpiling period and help ensure supply stability if the global energy crisis linked to the West Asia conflict involving the United States, Israel and Iran persists.
Concerns over the suitability of biodiesel for vehicles are also being addressed by the government, with Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir assuring that there are no issues with engine specifications, as Malaysia’s vehicle standards already provide for biodiesel blends of up to B20 since 2020.
Akmal Nasrullah said the use of B20 has already been introduced in several areas, including Langkawi, Labuan and Sarawak, without any major issues that could be cited in relation to alleged engine damage.
The National Biodiesel Programme began with B5 in 2011 before being expanded to B7 in 2014, followed by B10 and B20 in stages between 2019 and 2020.
Malaysia currently has 19 licensed biodiesel plants capable of supporting the implementation of B15 and the government’s long-term plans to raise the blend rate to B20, B30 and potentially B50, in line with the national green agenda.
In addition, the use of biodiesel produced from renewable sources is expected to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and support Malaysia’s commitment to low-carbon development and a more sustainable energy transition. — Bernama

Title: Sabah embarks on five-year initiative to document multi-ethnic heritage, says Hajiji
Article:
PENAMPANG, May 31 — The Sabah government is implementing a five-year initiative running until 2030 to document the establishment of the state’s multi-ethnic culture as a reference for future generations and as a tourism asset.
Chief Minister Datuk Seri Hajiji Noor said the documentation effort is important to ensure that ethnic heritage and traditions are not lost, while also strengthening the Kaamatan Festival as one of the main tourism products capable of elevating Sabah’s profile on the world stage.
“Kaamatan Festival is not just a cultural celebration, but an important platform to preserve ethnic traditions, foster tolerance, and strengthen political stability and state development.
“The presence of visitors from near and far makes this celebration special, with a diversity of sights, sounds, flavours and warm hospitality that we are all proud of,” he said. Sabah has more than 30 ethnic groups.
He said this when delivering a speech at the closing ceremony of the 2026 Sabah State-Level Kaamatan Festival, officiated by Sabah Yang Dipertua Negeri Tun Musa Aman at Hongkod Koisaan, Kadazandusun Cultural Association (KDCA) here today.
Also present were the Yang Dipertua Negeri’s wife, Toh Puan Faridah Tussin; Deputy Chief Minister I Datuk Seri Dr Joachim Gunsalam, who is also chairman of the State-Level Kaamatan Festival Main Organising Committee 2026; and KDCA president Tan Sri Joseph Pairin Kitingan.
Touching on the state’s development agenda, Hajiji said the government remains committed to ensuring sustainable development and the well-being of the people through the Sabah Maju Jaya (SMJ) 2.0 roadmap.
According to him, key focuses under the roadmap include creating more job opportunities, increasing agricultural sector productivity, empowering human capital, and safeguarding the welfare of both urban and rural communities.
He said that in line with this year’s theme, the people are urged to remain united in facing various challenges such as economic uncertainty and the rising cost of living.
“Ethnic diversity and cultural differences in Sabah should not be an obstacle; instead, they should form the basis of unity among the people and the development of the state.
“A bright future for Sabah cannot be delivered by political rhetoric, but through hard work, dedication, harmonious efforts and sincere cooperation from all levels of society,” he stressed. — Bernama

Title: KLIA Aerotrain returns to full 24-hour service from midnight, says transport minister
Article:
PUTRAJAYA, May 31 — The Aerotrain at KLIA Terminal 1 will resume full 24-hour operations starting 12am, June 1.
Transport Minister Anthony Loke said the service is now ready after months of rigorous safety upgrades and testing by Malaysia Airports Holdings Bhd in coordination with the Land Public Transport Agency (Apad) and verified by independent railway assessors.
The reinstatement follows the completion of a Comprehensive Action Plan (CAP) that began in November 2025.
The programme included detailed inspections, system testing, validation and extended trial runs under both passenger and non-passenger conditions.
Loke said that engineering maintenance will be carried out daily from 12am to 5am, with a single train remaining in service during those hours.
He added that Apad will maintain strict regulatory oversight to ensure the Aerotain’s long-term safety and reliability.

Title: PM calls for love, kindness and moderation in Wesak Day message
Article:
